text: 1. (a) Define a straight line. (b) What is an angle? (c) In an angle, what is the vertex?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Straight line (5 marks):
A straight line is defined as 'a line such that, if any portion of it is placed with its ends in the line, the entire portion so placed will lie in the line, however it may be applied.' This means that a straight line has no curvature; any part of it perfectly coincides with any other part. It is also called a right line. (Unit 1, Session 1)

(b) Angle (5 marks):
An angle is 'the figure formed by two straight lines which diverge from a point or converge to a point.' In simpler terms, it is the amount of turn between two rays that meet at a common point. (Unit 1, Session 1)

(c) Vertex (5 marks):
The vertex is the common point where the two lines (or rays) meet. It is the corner point of the angle. (Unit 1, Session 1)

text: 2. (a) Define a right angle and give its measure. (b) Define an acute angle and an obtuse angle.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Right angle (6 marks):
A right angle is defined as one‑quarter of a whole turn. It is the angle made by a horizontal line and a vertical line. Its measure is exactly 90°. Right angles are fundamental in geometry and are used to define perpendicular lines. (Unit 1, Session 1)

(b) Acute and obtuse angles (9 marks):
An acute angle is an angle that is less than a right angle, i.e., its measure is between 0° and 90°. For example, 30° and 45° are acute angles.
An obtuse angle is an angle that is greater than a right angle but less than two right angles, i.e., its measure is between 90° and 180°. For example, 120° and 150° are obtuse angles. (Unit 1, Session 1)

text: 3. (a) What are complementary angles? (b) What are supplementary angles? (c) Give one example of each.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Complementary angles (5 marks):
Two angles are called complementary if their sum is exactly a right angle, i.e., 90°. They do not have to be adjacent; they can be separate angles. (Unit 1, Session 1)

(b) Supplementary angles (5 marks):
Two angles are called supplementary if their sum is exactly two right angles, i.e., 180°. They do not have to be adjacent; they can be separate angles. (Unit 1, Session 1)

(c) Examples (5 marks):
Complementary: 30° and 60° because 30° + 60° = 90°.
Supplementary: 110° and 70° because 110° + 70° = 180°. (Unit 1, Session 1)

text: 4. (a) What are vertically opposite angles? (b) What is the sum of angles at a point?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Vertically opposite angles (8 marks):
Vertically opposite angles are the angles formed when two straight lines intersect. They are the angles opposite each other, and they are always equal. For example, if two lines intersect, the angles a and c are vertically opposite, and b and d are vertically opposite. This property is useful in solving many geometric problems. (Unit 1, Session 1)

(b) Sum of angles at a point (7 marks):
When several angles meet at a point (i.e., they share the same vertex and their arms cover the entire plane around the point), their sum is 360°. This is because they form a complete revolution around that point. (Unit 1, Session 2)

text: 5. (a) Define parallel lines. (b) What is a transversal? (c) State the property of corresponding angles.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Parallel lines (5 marks):
Parallel lines are two lines in a plane that never meet, no matter how far they are extended. They always maintain the same distance between them. (Unit 1, Session 2)

(b) Transversal (5 marks):
A transversal is a line that cuts (intersects) two or more lines. When a transversal crosses parallel lines, it creates various angles that have special relationships. (Unit 1, Session 2)

(c) Property of corresponding angles (5 marks):
Corresponding angles are angles that occupy the same relative position at each intersection where a transversal crosses parallel lines. They are equal in measure. For example, if a transversal intersects two parallel lines, the angles in the same corner at each intersection are equal. (Unit 1, Session 2)

text: 6. (a) What are alternate angles? (b) What are co‑interior angles? (c) State their properties.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Alternate angles (5 marks):
Alternate angles are the pair of angles that lie on opposite sides of the transversal and between the two parallel lines. For example, angles b and f are alternate angles, as are c and e. (Unit 1, Session 2)

(b) Co‑interior angles (5 marks):
Co‑interior angles are the pair of angles that lie on the same side of the transversal and between the two parallel lines. For example, angles b and e are co‑interior, as are c and f. (Unit 1, Session 2)

(c) Properties (5 marks):
Alternate angles are equal in measure. Co‑interior angles are supplementary, i.e., their sum is 180°. (Unit 1, Session 2)

text: 7. (a) What is a triangle? (b) What is the sum of the interior angles of a triangle? (c) State the exterior angle property of a triangle.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Triangle (4 marks):
A triangle is a simple closed curve formed by three line segments. It has three sides, three vertices, and three angles. It is one of the most basic polygons in geometry. (Unit 1, Session 3)

(b) Sum of interior angles (5 marks):
The sum of the interior angles of any triangle is always 180°. This is a fundamental property and can be proven by drawing a line parallel to one side through the opposite vertex. (Unit 1, Session 3)

(c) Exterior angle property (6 marks):
The exterior angle of a triangle is equal to the sum of the two opposite interior angles. For example, if we extend one side of a triangle, the angle formed outside the triangle is equal to the sum of the two interior angles that are not adjacent to it. (Unit 1, Session 3)

text: 8. (a) Describe an equilateral triangle. (b) Describe an isosceles triangle. (c) Describe a scalene triangle.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Equilateral triangle (5 marks):
An equilateral triangle has all sides equal in length and all interior angles equal to 60°. It has three lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of order 3. (Unit 1, Session 3)

(b) Isosceles triangle (5 marks):
An isosceles triangle has two equal sides and two equal interior angles. It has one line of symmetry that bisects the base and the opposite angle, and it has no rotational symmetry. (Unit 1, Session 3)

(c) Scalene triangle (5 marks):
A scalene triangle has all sides different in length and all interior angles different. It has no line symmetry and no rotational symmetry. (Unit 1, Session 3)

text: 9. (a) What is a quadrilateral? (b) What is the sum of the interior angles of a quadrilateral? (c) Name any four types of quadrilaterals.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Quadrilateral (4 marks):
A quadrilateral is a polygon with four edges (sides) and four vertices (corners). (Unit 1, Session 4)

(b) Sum of interior angles (5 marks):
The sum of the interior angles of a simple quadrilateral is 360°. (Unit 1, Session 4)

(c) Four types (6 marks):
Rectangle, square, rhombus, parallelogram, trapezium, kite. (Unit 1, Session 4)

text: 10. (a) State the properties of a rectangle. (b) State the properties of a square. (c) State the properties of a rhombus.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Rectangle (5 marks):
A rectangle has opposite sides equal, each angle 90°, opposite sides parallel, rotational symmetry of order 2, and diagonals that bisect each other and are equal in length. (Unit 1, Session 4)

(b) Square (5 marks):
A square has all sides equal, each angle 90°, opposite sides parallel, four lines of symmetry, diagonals equal and bisect each other at right angles, and rotational symmetry of order 4. (Unit 1, Session 4)

(c) Rhombus (5 marks):
A rhombus has all sides equal, opposite sides parallel, opposite angles equal, two lines of symmetry, rotational symmetry of order 2, and diagonals that bisect each other at right angles. (Unit 1, Session 4)

text: 11. (a) Define a parallelogram and list its properties. (b) Define a kite and list its properties.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Parallelogram (8 marks):
A parallelogram is a quadrilateral with opposite sides equal and parallel, and opposite angles equal. Its properties include: opposite angles are equal; two pairs of opposite sides are equal and parallel; the diagonals divide each other into two equal parts; and it has rotational symmetry of order 2. (Unit 1, Session 4)

(b) Kite (7 marks):
A kite is a quadrilateral with two pairs of adjacent sides equal. Its properties include: each pair of opposite angles are equal; one diagonal is a line of symmetry; no rotational symmetry; only one diagonal is bisected; diagonals cross at right angles. (Unit 1, Session 4)

text: 12. (a) Define a trapezium. (b) What is a polyhedron? (c) Give two examples of polyhedra.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Trapezium (5 marks):
A trapezium is a quadrilateral with one pair of parallel sides. (Unit 1, Session 4)

(b) Polyhedron (5 marks):
A polyhedron is a solid made up of only polygonal faces; the faces meet at edges which are line segments, and the edges meet at points called vertices. (Unit 1, Session 5)

(c) Examples (5 marks):
Cuboid, cube, triangular prism, pyramid. (Unit 1, Session 5)

text: 13. (a) What is a geometry net? (b) State Euler’s formula for solids. (c) What is Euler’s formula used for?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Geometry net (4 marks):
A geometry net is a two‑dimensional shape that can be folded to form a three‑dimensional shape or solid. (Unit 1, Session 5)

(b) Euler’s formula (5 marks):
For solids, V + F = E + 2, where V = vertices, F = faces, E = edges. (Unit 1, Session 5)

(c) Use (6 marks):
Euler's formula helps find the number of faces, edges, or vertices of a solid when the other two are known. For example, if you know a solid has 8 vertices and 6 faces, you can find the number of edges: E = V + F - 2 = 8 + 6 - 2 = 12. (Unit 1, Session 5)

text: 14. (a) Define congruent triangles. (b) Define similar triangles. (c) What is the key difference between congruent and similar?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Congruent triangles (5 marks):
Congruent triangles have both the same shape and the same size. (Unit 1, Session 6)

(b) Similar triangles (5 marks):
Similar triangles have the same shape, but not necessarily the same size. (Unit 1, Session 6)

(c) Difference (5 marks):
Congruent figures are identical in shape and size; similar figures have the same shape but may differ in size. (Unit 1, Session 6)

text: 15. (a) List the triangle congruence criteria. (b) What is the relationship between congruent and similar triangles?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Criteria (10 marks):
1. All three pairs of corresponding sides equal (SSS).
2. Two pairs of corresponding sides and the included angle equal (SAS).
3. Two pairs of corresponding angles and the included side equal (ASA).
4. Two pairs of corresponding angles and a pair of corresponding sides not between them equal (AAS).
5. In right triangles, the hypotenuse and one other side equal (RHS). (Unit 1, Session 6)

(b) Relationship (5 marks):
If two objects are congruent, they are also similar. (Unit 1, Session 6)

text: 16. (a) What is a line of symmetry? (b) How many lines of symmetry does a rectangle have? (c) How many does a square have?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Line of symmetry (5 marks):
A line of symmetry is a line that divides a figure into two identical halves that are mirror images. (Unit 1, Session 6)

(b) Rectangle (5 marks):
A rectangle has 2 lines of symmetry. (Unit 1, Session 6)

(c) Square (5 marks):
A square has 4 lines of symmetry. (Unit 1, Session 6)

text: 17. (a) Define rotational symmetry. (b) What is the order of rotational symmetry of a square? (c) What is the order for an equilateral triangle?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Rotational symmetry (5 marks):
Rotational symmetry is when a figure can be rotated and the same pattern is obtained again. (Unit 1, Session 6)

(b) Square (5 marks):
A square has rotational symmetry of order 4. (Unit 1, Session 6)

(c) Equilateral triangle (5 marks):
An equilateral triangle has rotational symmetry of order 3. (Unit 1, Session 6)

text: 18. (a) Describe the steps to bisect a straight line. (b) Describe the steps to construct a perpendicular at a point on a line.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Bisecting a line (7 marks):
1. Draw a straight line AB of any convenient length.
2. With the compass point at A and any reasonable radius (usually more than half of AB), draw an arc above and below the line AB.
3. Using the same radius and B as the centre, draw an arc above and below the line AB to intersect the first arcs at P and M.
4. With a ruler, join P to M. PM is the perpendicular bisector of AB. (Unit 2, Session 1)

(b) Perpendicular at a point on a line (8 marks):
1. Draw a line AB and mark a point M on it.
2. With M as the centre and any convenient radius, draw a semi‑circle around M to cut the line AB at P and Q.
3. With compass point at P and a convenient radius, draw an arc on any one side of the line.
4. Using the same radius and Q as the centre, draw another arc to intersect the first arc at X.
5. Join X to M with a straight line. Line MX is perpendicular to AB at M. (Unit 2, Session 1)

text: 19. (a) How do you construct a perpendicular from a point outside a line? (b) How do you construct a parallel line to a given line?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Perpendicular from external point (7 marks):
1. Draw a line AB and indicate a point M outside the line.
2. With M as the centre, draw arcs to cut the line AB at A and B.
3. Place the compass at A and draw an arc on the other side of the line in the opposite side of M.
4. Using B as the centre and the same radius, draw an arc to intersect the previous arc at K.
5. Draw a straight line to join M and K. This is the perpendicular from M to AB. (Unit 2, Session 1)

(b) Parallel line (8 marks):
1. Draw a line PQ and mark two points A and B on it.
2. With A as the centre and a convenient radius, draw a semi‑circle above PQ.
3. Using the same radius and B as the centre, draw another semi‑circle above PQ.
4. Draw a line to touch the turning points of the two semi‑circles at R and S. Line RS is parallel to line PQ. (Unit 2, Session 1)

text: 20. (a) How do you construct a 60° angle? (b) How do you construct a 30° angle? (c) How do you construct a 120° angle?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) 60° angle (5 marks):
1. Draw a line PQ and mark a point P on it.
2. With P as the centre and any convenient radius, draw a semi‑circle around P to cut PQ at X.
3. Using the same radius and the compass point at X, draw an arc to intersect the semi‑circle at Y.
4. Join P to Y with a straight line to produce ∠YPQ = 60°. (Unit 2, Session 2)

(b) 30° angle (5 marks):
First construct a 60° angle and then bisect it to get 30°. (Unit 2, Session 2)

(c) 120° angle (5 marks):
1. Draw a line PQ and mark a point P on it.
2. With P as the centre and any convenient radius, draw a semi‑circle around P to cut PQ at M and N.
3. With the same radius and N as the centre, draw an arc to cut the semi‑circle at A.
4. With A as the centre and using the same radius, draw another arc to cut the semi‑circle at V.
5. Join P to V with a straight line to get ∠VPQ = 120°. (Unit 2, Session 2)

text: 21. (a) How do you construct a 90° angle? (b) How do you construct a 135° angle?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) 90° angle (7 marks):
1. Draw a line PQ with an extension at P.
2. With P as the centre and any convenient radius, draw a semi‑circle around P to cut PQ at two points, N and M.
3. Using the same radius and with N and M as centres, draw arcs to cut the semi‑circle at A and B respectively.
4. Using the same radius and A as the centre, draw an arc above the semi‑circle. With B as the centre and the same radius, draw another arc to intersect the first arc at V.
5. Join V to P with a straight line such that ∠VPQ = 90°. (Unit 2, Session 2)

(b) 135° angle (8 marks):
135° = 90° + 45°. First construct a 90° angle and a 45° angle (by bisecting the other 90°), then combine them to get 135°. (Unit 2, Session 2)

text: 22. (a) Describe how to construct a triangle given the lengths of all three sides. (b) Describe how to construct a triangle given two sides and the included angle.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Three sides (7 marks):
1. Draw one side (say AB) of length equal to one of the given sides.
2. With A as the centre and radius equal to the second side, draw an arc above AB.
3. With B as the centre and radius equal to the third side, draw an arc to intersect the first arc at C.
4. Join A to C and B to C to complete triangle ABC. (Unit 2, Session 3)

(b) Two sides and included angle (8 marks):
1. Draw one side (say PQ) of the given length.
2. Construct the given angle at Q.
3. Mark the length of the second side along the arm of the angle to get point R.
4. Join P to R to complete triangle PQR. (Unit 2, Session 3)

text: 23. (a) Describe how to construct a triangle given two angles and the included side. (b) What is the first step in constructing a square?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Two angles and included side (7 marks):
1. Draw the given side (say AB).
2. Construct the two given angles at A and B so that their arms intersect at C.
3. Join A to C and B to C to complete triangle ABC. (Unit 2, Session 3)

(b) First step in constructing a square (8 marks):
Construct a line segment equal to the side length of the square. (Unit 2, Session 4)

text: 24. (a) Describe the steps to construct a rectangle. (b) Describe the steps to construct a parallelogram.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Rectangle (7 marks):
1. Construct a line AB of length equal to the length of the rectangle.
2. Construct perpendiculars at A and B.
3. With the compass point at A and radius equal to the breadth, draw an arc to cut the perpendicular at D.
4. Using the same radius and B as the centre, draw an arc to cut the perpendicular at C.
5. Join C to D to complete the rectangle ABCD. (Unit 2, Session 4)

(b) Parallelogram (8 marks):
1. Draw a line PQ of the given length.
2. Construct the given angle at P and mark the length of the adjacent side to get S.
3. With Q as the centre and radius equal to PS, draw an arc.
4. With S as the centre and radius equal to PQ, draw an arc to intersect the previous arc at R.
5. Join S to R and Q to R to obtain parallelogram PQRS. (Unit 2, Session 4)

text: 25. (a) How do you construct a regular hexagon? (b) What is a locus? (c) Give an example of a locus.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Regular hexagon (6 marks):
1. Draw a circle with centre O and radius equal to the side of the hexagon.
2. Make a point A on the circumference.
3. With the same radius and the compass point at A, mark point B on the circumference.
4. Continue marking points C, D, E, and F using the same radius.
5. Join the points in order: A‑B‑C‑D‑E‑F‑A to obtain a regular hexagon. (Unit 2, Session 4)

(b) Locus (5 marks):
A locus is the path traced by a point as it moves under certain specified conditions. (Unit 2, Session 5)

(c) Example (4 marks):
The locus of points equidistant from a fixed point is a circle. (Unit 2, Session 5)

text: 26. (a) What is the locus of points equidistant from two fixed points? (b) What is the locus of points equidistant from two intersecting lines?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Two fixed points (7 marks):
The locus is the perpendicular bisector of the line segment joining the two points. (Unit 2, Session 5)

(b) Two intersecting lines (8 marks):
The locus is the angle bisector of the angle formed by the two lines. (Unit 2, Session 5)

text: 27. (a) What is a circumscribed circle of a triangle? (b) How do you construct a circumscribed circle?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Circumscribed circle (5 marks):
A circumscribed circle is a circle that passes through all three vertices of a triangle. (Unit 2, Session 5)

(b) Construction (10 marks):
1. Construct the triangle ABC with the given conditions.
2. Bisect any two sides of the triangle.
3. The point of intersection of the bisectors is the centre of the circumscribed circle.
4. Use this centre and the distance to any vertex as the radius to draw the circle. (Unit 2, Session 5)

text: 28. (a) What is an inscribed circle of a triangle? (b) How do you construct an inscribed circle?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Inscribed circle (5 marks):
An inscribed circle is a circle that touches all three sides of a triangle. (Unit 2, Session 5)

(b) Construction (10 marks):
1. Construct the triangle ABC.
2. Bisect any two angles of the triangle.
3. The point of intersection of the angle bisectors is the centre of the inscribed circle.
4. From this centre, drop a perpendicular to one side to get the radius.
5. Using this centre and the radius, draw the circle. (Unit 2, Session 5)

text: 29. (a) What is a right‑angled triangle? (b) What is the hypotenuse? (c) In a right triangle, what are the opposite and adjacent sides?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Right‑angled triangle (4 marks):
A right‑angled triangle is a triangle with one interior angle equal to 90°. (Unit 3, Session 1)

(b) Hypotenuse (5 marks):
The hypotenuse is the longest side of a right‑angled triangle and is always opposite the right angle. (Unit 3, Session 1)

(c) Opposite and adjacent (6 marks):
In a right‑angled triangle, the side opposite a given acute angle is called the opposite side. The side adjacent to that angle (other than the hypotenuse) is called the adjacent side. (Unit 3, Session 1)

text: 30. (a) State Pythagoras’ theorem. (b) Write the formula for the theorem. (c) What is a Pythagorean triple?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Theorem (5 marks):
Pythagoras' theorem states that in a right‑angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides. (Unit 3, Session 1)

(b) Formula (5 marks):
If the sides are a, b, and hypotenuse c, then c² = a² + b². (Unit 3, Session 1)

(c) Pythagorean triple (5 marks):
A Pythagorean triple is a set of three whole numbers that satisfy Pythagoras' theorem, e.g., 3, 4, 5 (since 3² + 4² = 5²). (Unit 3, Session 1)

text: 31. (a) State the three trigonometric ratios. (b) Explain SOH‑CAH‑TOA. (c) Give the formula for tangent.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Three ratios (4 marks):
The three common trigonometric ratios are sine (sin), cosine (cos), and tangent (tan). (Unit 3, Session 2)

(b) SOH‑CAH‑TOA (6 marks):
SOH: Sine = Opposite / Hypotenuse.
CAH: Cosine = Adjacent / Hypotenuse.
TOA: Tangent = Opposite / Adjacent. (Unit 3, Session 2)

(c) Tangent formula (5 marks):
tan θ = Opposite / Adjacent. (Unit 3, Session 2)

text: 32. (a) What are inverse trigonometric functions? (b) Give an example of when they are used.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Inverse functions (8 marks):
Inverse trigonometric functions take side ratios and give angles. They are the inverses of sine, cosine, and tangent. Examples: sin⁻¹(x), cos⁻¹(x), tan⁻¹(x). (Unit 3, Session 2)

(b) Example (7 marks):
If we know the opposite and adjacent sides of a right‑angled triangle, we can find the angle θ using tan⁻¹(opposite/adjacent). (Unit 3, Session 2)

text: 33. (a) Define angle of elevation. (b) Define angle of depression. (c) With the aid of a sketch, illustrate each.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Angle of elevation (5 marks):
The angle of elevation is the angle between the horizontal line of sight and the object when a person looks up at an object. (Unit 3, Session 3)

(b) Angle of depression (5 marks):
The angle of depression is the angle between the horizontal line of sight and the object when a person looks down at an object. (Unit 3, Session 3)

(c) Sketch (5 marks):
For elevation: draw a horizontal line from the observer's eye to the object; the angle above the horizontal is the angle of elevation. For depression: draw a horizontal line from the eye; the angle below the horizontal is the angle of depression. (Unit 3, Session 3)

text: 34. (a) What is a vector quantity? (b) What is a scalar quantity? (c) Give two examples of each.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Vector (5 marks):
A vector quantity is a quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Examples: displacement, velocity, force. (Unit 4, Session 1)

(b) Scalar (5 marks):
A scalar quantity is a quantity that has only magnitude. Examples: time, speed, distance, mass. (Unit 4, Session 1)

(c) Examples (5 marks):
Vector: acceleration, momentum. Scalar: area, volume. (Unit 4, Session 1)

text: 35. (a) What is the zero vector? (b) What is the negative of a vector? (c) How do you find the magnitude of a vector (x, y)?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Zero vector (4 marks):
The zero vector is a vector with zero components (0, 0) and zero magnitude. (Unit 4, Session 1)

(b) Negative vector (5 marks):
The negative of a vector is a vector with the same magnitude but opposite direction. For vector **v**, its negative is -**v**. (Unit 4, Session 1)

(c) Magnitude (6 marks):
For a vector (x, y), its magnitude is |v| = √(x² + y²). (Unit 4, Session 1)

text: 36. (a) State the condition for two vectors to be equal. (b) How do you add two vectors? (c) How do you subtract two vectors?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Equal vectors (4 marks):
Two vectors are equal if they have the same magnitude and direction. In terms of components, their corresponding components must be equal. (Unit 4, Session 1)

(b) Addition (5 marks):
To add two vectors, add their corresponding components: (x₁, y₁) + (x₂, y₂) = (x₁+x₂, y₁+y₂). (Unit 4, Session 2)

(c) Subtraction (6 marks):
To subtract two vectors, subtract their corresponding components: (x₁, y₁) - (x₂, y₂) = (x₁-x₂, y₁-y₂). (Unit 4, Session 2)

text: 37. (a) What is the resultant of two vectors? (b) How do you find a perpendicular vector? (c) What is a position vector?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Resultant (5 marks):
The resultant of two vectors is the vector that starts from the starting point of the first and ends at the end point of the second when they are placed end‑to‑end. (Unit 4, Session 2)

(b) Perpendicular vector (5 marks):
A vector perpendicular to (a, b) can be found by rotating it through 90° or 270°, e.g., (-b, a) or (b, -a). (Unit 4, Session 3)

(c) Position vector (5 marks):
A position vector is a vector that represents the position of a point relative to an origin. It is denoted by a letter like **a**. (Unit 4, Session 3)

text: 38. (a) How do you find the vector joining two points A and B? (b) What is the midpoint theorem for vectors?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Vector AB (7 marks):
If A and B have position vectors a and b, then the vector joining A to B is AB = b - a. (Unit 4, Session 4)

(b) Midpoint theorem (8 marks):
The position vector of the midpoint of AB is half the sum of the position vectors of A and B: m = (a + b)/2. (Unit 4, Session 4)

text: 39. (a) What is a bearing? (b) How are bearings measured? (c) What is a back bearing?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Bearing (5 marks):
A bearing is an angle measured clockwise from the north direction. (Unit 4, Session 5)

(b) Measurement (5 marks):
Bearings are usually given in three digits, e.g., 025°. (Unit 4, Session 5)

(c) Back bearing (5 marks):
The back bearing is the bearing of the first point from the second point. For example, if the bearing of B from A is 025°, then the back bearing (bearing of A from B) is 205°. (Unit 4, Session 5)

text: 40. (a) Define the circumference of a circle. (b) Define radius and diameter. (c) Give the formula for circumference in terms of radius.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Circumference (5 marks):
The circumference of a circle is the total distance around the circle; it is its perimeter. (Unit 5, Session 1)

(b) Radius and diameter (5 marks):
The radius is the distance from the centre to any point on the circumference. The diameter is a line that divides the circle into two equal parts and is twice the radius. (Unit 5, Session 1)

(c) Formula (5 marks):
C = 2πr, where r is the radius. (Unit 5, Session 1)

text: 41. (a) Define a chord, an arc, a segment, and a sector of a circle. (b) What is a tangent?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Definitions (10 marks):
Chord: A straight line joining any two points on the circumference.
Arc: Any portion or part of the circumference.
Segment: The area bounded by a chord and the arc it cuts off.
Sector: An area bounded by two radii and the arc between them. (Unit 5, Session 1)

(b) Tangent (5 marks):
A tangent is a straight line that touches the circle at only one point and does not cut the circumference when extended. (Unit 5, Session 1)

text: 42. (a) Give the formula for the area of a circle. (b) Give the formula for the area of a semi‑circle. (c) Give the formula for the area of a quadrant.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Circle (5 marks):
A = πr². (Unit 5, Session 1)

(b) Semi‑circle (5 marks):
A = (½)πr². (Unit 5, Session 1)

(c) Quadrant (5 marks):
A = (¼)πr². (Unit 5, Session 1)

text: 43. (a) How do you find the length of an arc? (b) How do you find the area of a sector?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Arc length (7 marks):
Arc length = (θ/360) × 2πr, where θ is the angle subtended at the centre. (Unit 5, Session 2)

(b) Sector area (8 marks):
Area of sector = (θ/360) × πr². (Unit 5, Session 2)

text: 44. (a) Define a cylinder. (b) Give the volume of a cylinder. (c) Give the curved surface area of a cylinder.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Cylinder (4 marks):
A cylinder is a prism with a circular cross‑section. Examples include milk tins, oil drums, and fluorescent tubes. (Unit 5, Session 3)

(b) Volume (5 marks):
V = πr²h. (Unit 5, Session 3)

(c) Curved surface area (6 marks):
Curved surface area = 2πrh. (Unit 5, Session 3)

text: 45. (a) Give the total surface area of a closed cylinder. (b) Give the total surface area of an open cylinder.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Closed cylinder (7 marks):
Total surface area of a closed cylinder = 2πr² + 2πrh = 2πr(r + h). (Unit 5, Session 3)

(b) Open cylinder (8 marks):
Total surface area of an open cylinder (open at one end) = πr² + 2πrh. (Unit 5, Session 3)

text: 46. (a) What is a cone? (b) Give the volume of a cone. (c) Give the curved surface area of a cone.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Cone (4 marks):
A cone is a pyramid with a circular base. Examples include a hat, a yam mound. (Unit 5, Session 4)

(b) Volume (5 marks):
V = (1/3)πr²h. (Unit 5, Session 4)

(c) Curved surface area (6 marks):
Curved surface area = πrl, where l is the slant height. (Unit 5, Session 4)

text: 47. (a) Give the total surface area of a cone. (b) What is the relationship between l, r, and h in a cone?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Total surface area (7 marks):
Total surface area of a cone = πr² + πrl. (Unit 5, Session 4)

(b) Relationship (8 marks):
In a cone, the slant height l, radius r, and perpendicular height h are related by l² = r² + h². (Unit 5, Session 4)

text: 48. (a) Define a rectangular pyramid. (b) Give the volume of a rectangular pyramid. (c) Give the volume of a square pyramid.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Rectangular pyramid (4 marks):
A rectangular pyramid is a pyramid with a rectangular base and triangular slanting faces. (Unit 5, Session 4)

(b) Volume (5 marks):
Volume = (1/3) × base area × height. (Unit 5, Session 4)

(c) Square pyramid (6 marks):
Volume = (1/3) × side² × height. (Unit 5, Session 4)

text: 49. (a) Define a sphere. (b) Give the surface area of a sphere. (c) Give the volume of a sphere.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Sphere (4 marks):
A sphere is a solid that is round like a ball or an egg. (Unit 5, Session 4)

(b) Surface area (5 marks):
Surface area = 4πr². (Unit 5, Session 4)

(c) Volume (6 marks):
Volume = (4/3)πr³. (Unit 5, Session 4)

text: 50. (a) Define latitude. (b) What is the equator? (c) What is the range of latitudes?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Latitude (5 marks):
Latitudes are imaginary lines that run around the earth, perpendicular to the axis of the earth. (Unit 6, Session 1)

(b) Equator (5 marks):
The equator is the latitude (0°) that divides the earth into two equal parts; it is the only great circle among latitudes. (Unit 6, Session 1)

(c) Range (5 marks):
Latitudes range from 90°N to 90°S. (Unit 6, Session 1)

text: 51. (a) Define longitude. (b) What is the prime meridian? (c) What is the range of longitudes?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Longitude (5 marks):
Longitudes are circles passing through the north and south poles; they are half great circles. (Unit 6, Session 1)

(b) Prime meridian (5 marks):
The prime meridian is the main meridian of longitude, 0°, also called the Greenwich meridian. (Unit 6, Session 1)

(c) Range (5 marks):
Longitudes range from 180°E to 180°W. (Unit 6, Session 1)

text: 52. (a) What is a great circle? (b) What is a small circle? (c) Why is the equator a great circle?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Great circle (5 marks):
A great circle is a slice through the centre of a sphere; its radius equals the sphere's radius and divides the sphere into two equal parts. (Unit 6, Session 1)

(b) Small circle (5 marks):
A small circle is any slice not passing through the centre; its radius is smaller than the sphere's radius. (Unit 6, Session 1)

(c) Equator as great circle (5 marks):
The equator passes through the centre of the earth and divides it into two equal parts, hence it is a great circle. (Unit 6, Session 1)

text: 53. (a) How do you find the distance along a meridian? (b) How do you find the distance along a parallel of latitude?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Along a meridian (7 marks):
Since a meridian is a great circle, the distance = (θ/360) × 2πR, where θ is the difference in latitude and R is the Earth's radius. (Unit 6, Session 2)

(b) Along a parallel (8 marks):
Distance = (α/360) × 2πR cos(latitude), where α is the difference in longitude. (Unit 6, Session 3)

text: 54. (a) What is Greenwich Mean Time (GMT)? (b) What is the relationship between longitude and time?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) GMT (5 marks):
GMT is the local time at Greenwich (0° longitude). (Unit 6, Session 1)

(b) Longitude‑time relationship (10 marks):
The earth rotates 360° in 24 hours. So 1° corresponds to 4 minutes, and 15° corresponds to 1 hour. Places east of Greenwich are ahead in time; places west are behind. (Unit 6, Session 1)

text: 55. (a) Define statistics. (b) Name the two broad areas of statistics. (c) Define descriptive statistics.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Statistics (4 marks):
Statistics is a branch of science that deals with the collection, classification, analysis, and interpretation of data to make informed decisions. (Unit 7, Session 1)

(b) Two areas (5 marks):
Descriptive statistics and inferential statistics. (Unit 7, Session 1)

(c) Descriptive statistics (6 marks):
Descriptive statistics consists of collection, organization, summarization, and presentation of data. (Unit 7, Session 1)

text: 56. (a) What is a population? (b) What is a random sample? (c) Name two data collection methods.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Population (5 marks):
A population refers to all observations or measurements of interest; it is the totality of all subjects possessing certain common traits. (Unit 7, Session 1)

(b) Random sample (5 marks):
A random sample is a sample where every element in the population has an equal chance of being selected. (Unit 7, Session 1)

(c) Data collection methods (5 marks):
Interview, questionnaire, observation, experiment, tests, past records, focus group discussion. (Unit 7, Session 1)

text: 57. (a) Define mean, median, and mode. (b) How do you find the median of an even number of values?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Definitions (9 marks):
Mean: The sum of all values divided by the number of values.
Median: The middle value when data is arranged in order of magnitude.
Mode: The value that occurs most often. (Unit 7, Session 2)

(b) Median for even number (6 marks):
If there is an even number of values, take the two middle values and find their average. (Unit 7, Session 2)

text: 58. (a) Define quartiles. (b) What is the interquartile range (IQR)? (c) What is the semi‑interquartile range?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Quartiles (5 marks):
Quartiles are numbers that separate the data into four equal parts: Q1 (lower quartile), Q2 (median), Q3 (upper quartile). (Unit 7, Session 3)

(b) IQR (5 marks):
IQR = Q3 – Q1. It indicates the spread of the middle 50% of the data. (Unit 7, Session 3)

(c) Semi‑interquartile range (5 marks):
The semi‑interquartile range is half the IQR: (Q3 – Q1)/2. (Unit 7, Session 4)

text: 59. (a) Define probability. (b) What is a sample space? (c) What is an event?
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Probability (5 marks):
Probability is the study of events that may or may not occur, assigning a numerical value to the likelihood of an event. (Unit 8, Session 1)

(b) Sample space (5 marks):
The sample space is the set of all possible outcomes of an experiment. (Unit 8, Session 1)

(c) Event (5 marks):
An event is a subset of the sample space. (Unit 8, Session 1)

text: 60. (a) State the addition rule for mutually exclusive events. (b) State the addition rule for non‑mutually exclusive events. (c) State the multiplication rule for independent events.
answer:
MODEL ANSWER (15 Marks)

(a) Mutually exclusive (5 marks):
For mutually exclusive events, P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). (Unit 8, Session 4)

(b) Non‑mutually exclusive (5 marks):
For non‑mutually exclusive events, P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) – P(A and B). (Unit 8, Session 4)

(c) Independent events (5 marks):
For independent events, P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B). (Unit 8, Session 5)
